title_en: Knowing the Prophet: His Mission, Status, and What He Brought
title_ar: معرفة النبي: بعثته ومكانته وما جاء به
content_en:
The second great obligatory knowledge that Ibn Abd al-Wahhab addresses is knowing the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. This chapter covers three areas: who the Prophet was as a person, the nature and scope of his prophetic mission, and what he brought in terms of guidance.

The Sheikh begins with the basic biographical facts that every Muslim must know: Muhammad ibn Abdullah ibn Abd al-Muttalib was born in Makkah, grew up an orphan, worked as a merchant renowned for honesty, and received his first revelation from Allah at the age of forty, in the Cave of Hira on Jabal an-Nur, through the angel Jibril. He preached openly for thirteen years in Makkah, then migrated to Madinah where he established the first Muslim community-state. He died at the age of sixty-three in Madinah, his mission complete.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab then turns to the doctrinal significance of knowing the Prophet. The Quran declares: 'Obey Allah and obey the messenger' (4:59) — a command that appears repeatedly and that establishes the Prophet's obedience as an independent religious obligation, not merely a corollary of obeying the Quran. The Prophet himself said: 'Whoever obeys me has obeyed Allah, and whoever disobeys me has disobeyed Allah.' This means that the Sunnah — the Prophet's words, actions, and tacit approvals — is a binding source of divine guidance, second only to the Quran.

The Sheikh addresses a critical matter that was directly relevant to the corruptions of his era: the Prophet's status as the final messenger. Allah declared: 'Muhammad is not the father of any of your men, but he is the Messenger of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets' (33:40). This means no prophet will come after him, and anyone who claims prophethood after him is a liar and an imposter. It also means that the religion is complete: 'This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you' (5:3).

A corollary of this completeness is that no act of worship is valid except what was established by the Prophet. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab is emphatic on this point: introducing new acts of worship not practiced by the Prophet — even with good intentions — is an innovation (bid'ah) that the Prophet explicitly warned against. The Prophet said: 'Whoever introduces into this matter of ours something that is not from it, it will be rejected.' This principle is the foundation of the Sheikh's critique of the bid'ah practices widespread in his time.

The chapter also covers what the Prophet brought in terms of content: a comprehensive way of life (deen) encompassing belief (aqeedah), worship (ibadah), law (shari'ah), character (akhlaq), and social relations (mu'amalat). Islam is not merely a personal spirituality but a complete guidance for the individual, the family, the community, and the state.

Finally, the Sheikh discusses love of the Prophet as a religious obligation. The Prophet said: 'None of you truly believes until I am more beloved to him than his child, his father, and all of mankind.' This love is expressed not through exaggerated praise that oversteps bounds — the Prophet warned: 'Do not praise me as the Christians praised Isa ibn Maryam' — but through following his Sunnah, defending his honor, and spreading his authentic teachings.
